us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes After Colonel Rescue
us-politics.body:
The US rescued a downed airman from behind enemy lines in Iran on Easter Sunday, with the CIA running a deception campaign to support the operation. Trump followed the news with a profanity-laced social media post threatening to bomb Iran's power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened.

Supporters say the rescue shows US military capability and that Trump's pressure is working. Critics, including a former Trump adviser, warn the threats make a negotiated deal less likely. One House Republican argued any US ground presence should be limited strictly to securing enriched uranium.

A retired Army general predicted the conflict could wrap up within 60 days. Meanwhile, Iran's economy is reportedly collapsing toward a barter system, and jet fuel costs are spiking globally, squeezing airlines and travelers.

sources.title: House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
Inside the daring rescue of airman behind enemy lines: How CIA assisted with 'deception campaign'
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Retired Army general predicts Iran war wrapped up in ‘less than 60 days’
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict
Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
Trump unleashes curse-filled social media rant at Iran after U.S. rescues colonel

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, NATO Strains Deepen Over Iran Crisis
us-politics.body:
Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi, a move Democrats say exposes the vulnerability of the position to presidential pressure. Senator Tim Kaine called it a warning to whoever comes next. NPR reported the firing alongside Trump moving to walk back several campaign promises.

Separately, Trump's handling of the Iran conflict is straining NATO. Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace has prompted analysts to question the durability of the alliance. The Hill's reporting suggests Trump has threatened withdrawal from NATO commitments as pressure mounts over the Strait of Hormuz.

On the domestic front, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries called on Speaker Johnson to end the House recess amid a lapse in DHS funding. Johnson has not responded publicly.

sources.title: Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse
Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee
Senate Democrat says Trump ‘needs a lot of prayer’
Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace as US Politics Turns Inward
us-politics.body:
Pope Leo used his Easter address to deliver a broad call for peace, with the Washington Post noting the message carried implicit weight given active US military operations in Iran. The speech drew wide attention across the political spectrum.

Back in Washington, a new ad campaign backed by Republicans is pushing back against Trump's stated interest in federalizing elections, warning it would undermine state control of voting. A federal judge also blocked a Trump administration effort to collect race-based admissions data from universities, a ruling Fox News covered as a setback for the administration's education agenda.

Fox News separately flagged Kamala Harris's recent travel and public comments as early signals of a 2028 presidential run, a read other outlets have not yet confirmed.

sources.title: On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war
Federal judge blocks Trump push to collect race-based admissions data
New ad campaign speaks out against Trump’s threat to ‘federalize’ elections
Kamala Harris' travels and comments clearly point to 2028

us-politics.headline: Iran War Reshapes Energy Debate and Hospital Safety Concerns Rise
us-politics.body:
The conflict in Iran is fueling a broader domestic debate about US energy policy. Opinion writers at The Hill argued the war demonstrates the strategic risk of relying on any single energy source, making the case for diversifying toward renewables alongside traditional fuels. The White House, meanwhile, reaffirmed its commitment to lunar resource development under the Artemis program.

On a separate domestic front, a Hill opinion piece raised concerns about hospital corporatization and its effect on patient safety, arguing that profit-driven consolidation is eroding care standards. A product safety recall also emerged, with roughly 740,000 frying pans sold at Walmart and Costco flagged as potential burn hazards.

sources.title: The war in Iran is showing why the US needs diverse energy resources
When hospitals become corporations, what happens to patient safety?
The White House vows America will never give up the moon again
740K pans sold at Walmart, Costco may pose burn hazards

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites Farmers as EV Demand Surges
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel crisis is hitting rural communities hard, with farmers reporting they cannot afford to put food on the table as fuel costs spiral. The squeeze is reshaping consumer behaviour in cities too, where secondhand electric vehicle prices are climbing as buyers rush to ditch combustion engines.

The divergence is stark. Urban Australians with the means to switch are doing so quickly, while those in regional areas - dependent on fuel for machinery and transport - have no easy alternative. The crisis is exposing a two-speed economy where geography determines who absorbs the pain.

sources.title: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits
'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table

au-politics.headline: Tasmanian Forest Logged Despite Recorded Swift Parrot Habitat
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest where the endangered swift parrot's call was recorded dozens of times has been logged, raising fresh questions about how wildlife data is used in forestry approvals. The swift parrot is critically endangered, with habitat loss identified as a primary driver of its decline.

Conservationists argue the logging demonstrates that environmental protections are failing in practice, even when evidence of threatened species is documented. Forestry interests maintain that approvals followed legal processes. The case is likely to intensify debate over Tasmania's native forest industry ahead of any federal review.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged

au-politics.headline: Family Court Orders Return of Aboriginal Child Moved 1,700km
au-politics.body:
A family court has ruled that an Aboriginal child removed from a remote Northern Territory community and relocated 1,700 kilometres away must be returned. The decision highlights ongoing tensions between child protection authorities and Indigenous families over removal practices that critics say echo the policies of the Stolen Generations.

The case raises serious questions about how welfare decisions are made for remote Indigenous children and whether cultural connection is given adequate legal weight. Supporters of the family say the removal was unjustified. Authorities have not publicly detailed their reasoning. The ruling adds pressure on governments to reform child protection frameworks in Indigenous communities.

sources.title: Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: US Airman Rescued from Iran as Nuclear Talks Shift
au-politics.body:
American special forces rescued a US F-15 airman from Iran in a covert operation, a significant escalation of direct US military activity inside Iranian territory. The rescue comes as Iran separately signalled openness to renewed diplomacy with the Trump administration, offering what analysts describe as a calibrated olive branch.

The two developments sit in tension. A special forces incursion is not the backdrop either side would typically choose for negotiations. Whether Washington treats the rescue as a closed incident or a provocation will shape whether the diplomatic opening holds. The situation remains fluid.

sources.title: Iran has offered Trump an olive branch
US F-15 airman rescued in Iran after special forces operation

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Closure Shocks Global Markets
world.body:
Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz and struck an industrial zone in southern Israel, while continuing missile and drone attacks on Gulf states including Kuwait, where power and water facilities were damaged. Trump threatened Iran with "hell" as a diplomatic deadline passed, but offered no concrete plan to reopen the waterway that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Europe is scrambling. Nepal has already announced two-day weekends to conserve fuel.

A US F-15E was shot down over Iran, with the seriously wounded pilot rescued in a covert operation Trump confirmed publicly. Iran's former foreign minister Zarif has proposed a peace roadmap, and Oman is mediating talks on Hormuz transit. Political scientist Mearsheimer sees no quick exit. Iran's World Cup participation is now also in doubt, with its minister citing the conflict as grounds to relocate matches away from the US.

sources.title: Seriously wounded US airman rescue from inside Iran, Trump says
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel
How downed F-15 US airman was rescued inside Iran
Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Iran’s World Cup participation depends on relocation from US, minister says
Why an attack on Bushehr nuclear plant would be catastrophic for the Gulf
Nepal announces two-day weekends as fuel crisis caused by Iran war deepens
Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe
Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust
Oman, Iran discuss smooth transit in Strait of Hormuz, Muscat says
“We blame Trump”: Drivers line up for free gas as prices surge in the US
US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states

world.headline: Israel Strikes Lebanon, Ben-Gvir Pushes Palestinian Death Row
world.body:
Israeli strikes killed at least 14 people across Lebanon on Easter Sunday, displacing Christian families during one of the most significant days in their calendar. The strikes mark a continued expansion of Israeli military operations beyond Gaza.

Inside Israel's government, National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir outlined plans to introduce a death row system for Palestinian prisoners. Separately, footage emerged showing an Israeli military dog attacking a Palestinian man inside a mosque. Ukraine meanwhile struck Russian oil infrastructure, hitting a port in Primorsk and a refinery in Nizhny Novgorod.

sources.title: At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon
Israel’s Ben-Gvir details plans for Palestinian ‘death row’
Christian Lebanese families displaced as they celebrate Easter
Footage shows Israeli military dog attacking Palestinian man inside mosque
Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

world.headline: Pope Leo Urges Peace at First Easter Mass
world.body: Pope Leo delivered his first Easter Mass, calling on world leaders to choose peace and warning that the world is growing dangerously indifferent to violence. The address came as conflicts raged across the Middle East, Eastern Europe, and beyond. It was a pointed debut for the new pontiff.

sources.title: Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence
Pope Leo calls for global leaders to choose peace in his first Easter Mass

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Photographs Moon; Bangladesh Measles Kills 100 Children
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew, including Canadian Jeremy Hansen - the first Canadian to travel to the Moon - sent back striking photographs of Earth and the lunar surface, blurring the line between scientific documentation and spectacle. The mission marks a milestone in crewed deep-space exploration.

On the ground, a suspected measles outbreak in Bangladesh has killed nearly 100 children, raising urgent questions about vaccination coverage. Separately, Hungary alleged a plot to blow up a gas pipeline ahead of national elections, and the Democratic Republic of Congo agreed to accept third-country deportees from the United States under a new deal with the Trump administration.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Dozens missing after migrant boat capsizes in Mediterranean
Earth in rear-view of Artemis II astronauts
DR Congo to receive ‘third-country’ deportees from the US under new deal
Artemis's stunning Moon pictures - science or holiday photos?
Suspected measles outbreak kills nearly 100 children in Bangladesh
Jeremy Hansen is the first Canadian to head to the moon

ai.headline: Suno's AI Music Tool Is a Copyright Lawsuit Waiting to Happen
ai.body:
Suno lets users generate music that sounds uncannily like real artists, and The Verge's deep dive makes clear the legal exposure is severe. The platform can produce convincing covers and stylistic imitations without licensing the underlying works, putting it squarely in the crosshairs of ongoing industry litigation against AI music generators.

The broader problem is structural. Suno's training data almost certainly includes copyrighted recordings, and its outputs can closely replicate protected styles and melodies. Rights holders are watching, and courts are still working out where the lines fall. Suno may be building a product on a foundation that could be legally dismantled beneath it.

sources.title: Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: OpenAI Moves Codex to Metered Pricing for All Users
ai.body:
OpenAI is ending any flat-rate access to Codex, its AI coding tool, and shifting all users to API-based consumption pricing. The move signals OpenAI's intent to monetize developer tools more aggressively as competition in the coding assistant space intensifies from rivals like GitHub Copilot and Anthropic's Claude.

For heavy users, costs could climb fast. Metered pricing rewards light usage but punishes teams that have built Codex into automated workflows. The timing is notable given that a developer on Hacker News separately documented building a full project in three months using AI tools that would have taken eight years solo - exactly the kind of intensive use case that will now carry a steeper bill.

sources.title: Codex is switching to API pricing based usage for all users
Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI

ai.headline: Artemis II Crew Rounds the Moon as SpaceX Eyes Orbital Data Centers
ai.body:
The Artemis II crew captured the first live footage of the Moon's far side this week, a milestone that underscores how routine deep-space human flight is becoming. The mission is reportedly going smoothly enough that the most newsworthy detail is a frozen urine management problem aboard the capsule.

Meanwhile, TechCrunch reports that SpaceX is exploring orbital data centers as a way to justify its enormous valuation. The idea is speculative but not absurd - low-latency compute in orbit has niche appeal for defense and scientific applications. Whether it can move the needle on a valuation already priced for dominance is a harder sell.

sources.title: Can orbital data centers help justify a massive valuation for SpaceX?
Artemis II crew see first glimpse of far side of Moon [video]
Artemis II is going so well that we're left to talk about frozen urine

ai.headline: Japan's Robot Workforce and Gemini's Map Skills Signal AI's Practical Turn
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots in roles that human workers simply won't take, from warehouse logistics to elder care. TechCrunch finds the results genuinely promising - not robots replacing workers, but filling a structural labor gap that demographics made inevitable. It is a quieter story than the displacement narrative, but arguably more consequential.

On the consumer side, Google's Gemini integration in Maps is drawing cautiously positive reviews. The Verge's hands-on found it useful for day planning in a way that felt practical rather than gimmicky. Taken together, both stories point to the same shift: AI earning trust through mundane competence rather than headline-grabbing capability.

sources.title: I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

btc.headline: North Korea Spent Six Months Draining $270M from Drift
btc.body:
The $270 million exploit of Solana-based trading platform Drift was a sustained North Korean intelligence operation, not an opportunistic hack. Preliminary findings show attackers spent roughly six months embedding themselves before executing the theft, planting insiders and mapping the protocol's vulnerabilities methodically.

The scale and patience of the operation mark it as one of the most sophisticated DeFi attacks on record. It reinforces a pattern: state-sponsored groups are no longer smashing windows. They are picking locks slowly, and most protocols are not watching closely enough to notice.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin Holds at $67K While Sentiment Craters
btc.body:
Bitcoin is stuck near $67,000 and some traders see lower lows as inevitable, yet the price has refused to collapse despite sentiment hitting its worst levels since the Iran war began. The divergence is notable. Historically, extreme negative sentiment has preceded recoveries, not further selloffs.

One reason for the resilience may be structural. ETF inflows mean institutional money now positions ahead of Federal Reserve decisions rather than reacting after the fact, effectively front-running macro catalysts. That changes the timing of Bitcoin's moves relative to traditional markets. Robert Kiyosaki, meanwhile, is using the gloom to repeat his case for Bitcoin and gold as the dollar's long-term decline continues.

sources.title: Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle

btc.headline: AI Is Breaking Crypto Security and Building New Rails
btc.body:
Ledger's CTO is warning that AI is accelerating crypto's security crisis, making phishing attacks more convincing, exploit code easier to generate, and social engineering harder to detect. The threat is not theoretical. Sophisticated attacks like the Drift operation show how much damage patient, well-resourced adversaries can do.

At the same time, Ant Group's blockchain arm is pushing in the opposite direction, launching a platform that lets AI agents transact autonomously on crypto rails. The bet is that AI and blockchain are complementary. The risk is that the same tools enabling autonomous transactions also make it easier to steal from them.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails

btc.headline: Token Oversupply Is Quietly Killing Crypto's Value Case
btc.body:
A growing body of analysis points to an existential problem in crypto markets: token supply is expanding far faster than genuine value creation. Projects launch tokens to fund themselves, insiders dump on retail, and prices drift lower even when underlying usage grows. The cycle is eroding trust across the broader market.

This structural issue sits beneath the current price stagnation. Bitcoin's relative stability masks a weaker picture in altcoins, where inflation from vesting schedules and new issuance is a constant headwind. Until projects tie token supply more tightly to real economic activity, the problem compounds.

sources.title: ‘Existential’ token problem seen as supply outpaces value creation

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory tart with caramelized leeks and shiitake mushrooms bound by a white miso butter glaze on a rough puff pastry base.
food.tip: Chill your pastry dough for at least 30 minutes before rolling — cold fat creates the flaky layers that make a galette worth the effort.

exercise.name: The Farmer's Sunday Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two heavy bags, backpack, or actual dumbbells
exercise.description: A loaded carry and functional strength session built around farmer walks, suitcase carries, and stair climbs with weight — training grip, posture, and real-world endurance in a single loop around your home or block.

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to human cells, memories, and beliefs, it becomes a sharp question about whether 'you' today are the same person you were a decade ago.
learn.why: On a quiet Sunday it's a rare chance to sit with a question that has no clean answer, one that threads through philosophy, neuroscience, and law — and quietly reframes how you think about change, continuity, and who you're becoming.
